question_text: Which type of friction is generally the largest in magnitude?
explanation: Static friction is the largest among the types of friction. The maximum value of static friction (limiting friction) is greater than kinetic friction, which in turn is greater than rolling friction. This is why it is harder to start moving an object than to keep it moving.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: Kinetic friction is less than limiting static friction. Once an object starts moving, the friction decreases.
Rolling friction is the smallest of the three contact friction types, which is why wheels are used for transport.
Fluid friction (viscous drag) depends on speed and medium, and is not generally compared in this hierarchy of contact friction.

question_text: The coefficient of static friction between two surfaces depends on:
explanation: The coefficient of friction depends on the nature of the surfaces in contact (material, roughness, etc.). It does not depend on the area of contact, the weight of the object, or the relative velocity. This is one of the fundamental laws of friction.
subject: physics

options.text: The area of contact between the surfaces
The nature of the surfaces in contact
The weight of the heavier object
The relative velocity between the surfaces

elimination_hints.hint: According to the laws of friction, the friction force is independent of the apparent area of contact between surfaces.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking friction depends on contact area

elimination_hints.hint: The coefficient of friction is a dimensionless ratio that characterizes the surface pair; it does not depend on the weight or normal force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the friction force (which depends on N) with the coefficient of friction

elimination_hints.hint: The coefficient of static friction applies before relative motion begins, so velocity is not a factor. Even kinetic friction coefficient is approximately independent of velocity.

question_text: A block is placed on a horizontal surface. A horizontal force is gradually increased from zero. The friction force acting on the block:
explanation: When the applied force is below the limiting friction, the static friction self-adjusts to be equal to the applied force (keeping the block stationary). It increases linearly with the applied force until it reaches its maximum value (limiting friction = μ_s N). Beyond that, the block starts moving and kinetic friction (a constant value less than limiting friction) acts.
subject: physics

options.text: Remains constant at μₛ N throughout
Is always zero until the block starts moving
Increases with applied force up to a maximum, then drops to a constant value
Increases indefinitely with the applied force

elimination_hints.hint: Static friction is not always at its maximum value. It adjusts to match the applied force and only reaches μₛN at the point of impending motion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking static friction is always equal to μₛN

elimination_hints.hint: Static friction is not zero; it acts as soon as an external force is applied and prevents the block from moving.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking friction only exists during motion

elimination_hints.hint: Friction has a maximum limit. Once the applied force exceeds limiting friction, the object moves and kinetic friction (a lower constant value) acts.

question_text: The angle of repose is defined as the angle of an inclined plane at which a body placed on it:
explanation: The angle of repose is the maximum angle of an inclined plane with the horizontal at which a body placed on it just begins to slide down. At this angle, the component of gravitational force along the plane equals the maximum static friction. It is related to the coefficient of static friction by tan θ = μₛ.
subject: physics

options.text: Remains at rest permanently regardless of angle

elimination_hints.hint: The angle of repose concerns the onset of downward sliding, not upward motion. Moving up would require an external force.
Uniform acceleration down the plane occurs when the angle exceeds the angle of repose. At the angle of repose, the body is on the verge of sliding.
The body remains at rest only for angles less than the angle of repose. At the angle of repose, it is at the tipping point of sliding.

question_text: A block of mass 10 kg is placed on a rough horizontal surface (μₛ = 0.4, μₖ = 0.3). A horizontal force of 30 N is applied. The friction force acting on the block is: (Take g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: Maximum static friction = μₛ × N = 0.4 × 100 = 40 N. Since the applied force (30 N) is less than the maximum static friction (40 N), the block does not move. The static friction adjusts to equal the applied force, so friction = 30 N.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: 40 N is the maximum static friction (μₛN), but friction only reaches this value at the point of impending motion. Here, 30 N < 40 N, so friction equals the applied force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Always using f = μN regardless of whether the block moves

elimination_hints.hint: 35 N does not correspond to any correct calculation. The friction self-adjusts to exactly balance the applied force when below the limiting value.
25 N is less than the applied force and would result in a net force, causing acceleration. But since the block can remain stationary (30 < 40), friction must equal 30 N.

question_text: A body of mass 5 kg is placed on a rough inclined plane of angle 30° with μₖ = 0.2. The acceleration of the body sliding down the plane is: (Take g = 10 m/s², cos 30° = 0.866, sin 30° = 0.5)
explanation: For a body sliding down a rough incline: a = g(sin θ − μₖ cos θ) = 10(0.5 − 0.2 × 0.866) = 10(0.5 − 0.1732) = 10 × 0.3268 = 3.27 m/s² ≈ 3.3 m/s². Kinetic friction acts up the incline opposing the downward sliding motion.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: 1.7 m/s² would be obtained from gμₖcosθ alone, which is just the deceleration due to friction and not the net acceleration down the incline.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using only the friction component instead of net force

elimination_hints.hint: 5.0 m/s² = g sin 30° is the acceleration on a frictionless incline. Friction reduces this value.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring friction on the inclined plane

elimination_hints.hint: 6.7 m/s² = g(sin θ + μ cos θ) would apply if friction acted downward (i.e., the body were pushed up), not when sliding down.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding friction component instead of subtracting it for downward motion

question_text: A 20 kg box is pushed along a rough horizontal floor with a constant velocity by applying a horizontal force. If μₖ = 0.25, the applied force is: (Take g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: When the box moves with constant velocity, acceleration is zero. By Newton's second law, the applied force equals the kinetic friction force. F = fₖ = μₖ × N = μₖ × mg = 0.25 × 20 × 10 = 50 N.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: 100 N would imply μₖ = 0.5 or some calculation error. With μₖ = 0.25 and N = 200 N, friction is 50 N.
25 N is half the correct value. Make sure to use N = mg = 20 × 10 = 200 N, not 100 N.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using incorrect value of normal force

elimination_hints.hint: 200 N is the normal force (mg), not the friction force. The friction force is μₖ times the normal force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing normal force with friction force

question_text: The coefficient of static friction between a block and an inclined plane is 1/√3. The maximum angle to which the inclined plane can be tilted without the block sliding is:
explanation: At the angle of repose, tan θ = μₛ = 1/√3. Therefore θ = tan⁻¹(1/√3) = 30°. This is the maximum angle at which the block remains stationary. Beyond this angle, the gravitational component along the plane exceeds the maximum static friction.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: tan 15° ≈ 0.27 ≠ 1/√3 ≈ 0.577. This angle is too small and would not utilize the full static friction.
tan 45° = 1, which would require μₛ = 1. Since μₛ = 1/√3 ≈ 0.577 < 1, the block would slide well before 45°.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing tan⁻¹(1) with tan⁻¹(1/√3)

elimination_hints.hint: tan 60° = √3 ≈ 1.73, requiring μₛ = √3, which is much larger than the given value of 1/√3.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the reciprocal of the tangent relationship

question_text: A horizontal force of 20 N just slides a block of mass 4 kg on a rough horizontal surface. The coefficient of static friction is: (Take g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: At the point of just sliding, the applied force equals the maximum static friction: F = μₛ × mg. So 20 = μₛ × 4 × 10 = 40μₛ. Therefore μₛ = 20/40 = 0.5.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: μₛ = 0.2 gives maximum friction = 0.2 × 40 = 8 N, which is much less than 20 N. The block would have started moving much earlier.
μₛ = 0.3 gives maximum friction = 0.3 × 40 = 12 N, still less than 20 N.
μₛ = 0.8 gives maximum friction = 0.8 × 40 = 32 N, meaning a 20 N force would not be sufficient to slide the block.

question_text: A body of mass 2 kg is placed on a rough horizontal surface. The coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.4. A force of 20 N is applied horizontally. The acceleration of the body is: (Take g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: Normal force N = mg = 2 × 10 = 20 N. Kinetic friction fₖ = μₖN = 0.4 × 20 = 8 N. First verify the block moves: the applied force (20 N) exceeds kinetic friction (8 N). Net force = 20 − 8 = 12 N. Acceleration a = 12/2 = 6 m/s².
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: 2 m/s² would be the acceleration if the full 20 N were reduced by 16 N of friction, which would require μₖ = 0.8, not 0.4.
4 m/s² = 8/2 would result from using only the friction force divided by mass, ignoring the applied force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using friction force alone instead of net force

elimination_hints.hint: 5 m/s² would be 10/2, not matching any correct combination of forces in this problem.

question_text: A block of mass m rests on a rough inclined plane of angle θ. The minimum force F applied parallel to the incline (up the slope) required to just move the block up the incline is:
explanation: To just move the block up the incline, F must overcome both the gravity component along the plane (mg sin θ) and the maximum static friction (μₛ mg cos θ) which acts downward (opposing the intended upward motion). Therefore F = mg sin θ + μₛ mg cos θ = mg(sin θ + μₛ cos θ).
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: mg sin θ is the gravity component along the incline but ignores friction. On a rough surface, friction must also be overcome.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring friction when calculating force on rough incline

elimination_hints.hint: mg(sin θ − μₛ cos θ) applies when the block is on the verge of sliding down (friction acts up the plane). For pushing up, friction acts down the plane and must be added.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the wrong direction for friction when pushing upward

elimination_hints.hint: μₛ mg cos θ is only the friction force. The gravitational component (mg sin θ) along the incline must also be overcome.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting the gravity component along the incline

question_text: A block of mass 2 kg is pressed against a rough vertical wall by a horizontal force of 40 N. If μₛ = 0.5 and μₖ = 0.4, the frictional force acting on the block is: (Take g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: The weight of the block = mg = 2 × 10 = 20 N (acts downward). The normal force on the block from the wall = 40 N (horizontal). Maximum static friction = μₛ × N = 0.5 × 40 = 20 N. Since the weight (20 N) equals the maximum static friction (20 N), the block is on the verge of sliding but doesn't slide. The friction force = 20 N (upward, balancing gravity).
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: 16 N = μₖ × N = 0.4 × 40, which is the kinetic friction. But the block is not sliding (weight ≤ maximum static friction), so kinetic friction does not apply.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using kinetic friction when the block is not moving

elimination_hints.hint: 40 N is the horizontal applied force (which creates the normal force), not the friction force. Friction acts vertically here to support the block's weight.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing normal force with friction force

elimination_hints.hint: 8 N doesn't balance the 20 N weight. Friction must equal 20 N to keep the block in equilibrium since friction is the only upward force.

question_text:
Assertion (A): It is easier to pull a lawn roller than to push it on a rough horizontal ground.
Reason (R): When pulling at an angle, the vertical component of the applied force reduces the normal force, thereby reducing the friction force.
explanation: Both A and R are correct, and R is the correct explanation of A. When pulling at an angle above the horizontal, the vertical component of the pulling force acts upward, reducing the effective normal force (N = mg − F sin θ). This decreases friction (f = μN). When pushing at a downward angle, the vertical component adds to the normal force (N = mg + F sin θ), increasing friction.
subject: physics

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason directly explains the Assertion: pulling reduces normal force and hence friction, which is exactly why pulling is easier.
The Reason is scientifically accurate: pulling at an angle does reduce the normal force component, which reduces friction.
The Assertion is correct and experimentally verifiable: pulling a roller at an angle requires less force than pushing it.

question_text:
Assertion (A): The coefficient of kinetic friction is always less than the coefficient of static friction for a given pair of surfaces.
Reason (R): Static friction is a self-adjusting force that can take any value from zero to the limiting value.
explanation: Both A and R are correct, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A. The assertion is true: μₖ < μₛ because once relative motion begins, the interlocking between surface irregularities is reduced. The reason is also true: static friction is indeed self-adjusting. However, the self-adjusting nature of static friction does not explain why μₖ < μₛ; the explanation lies in the microscopic surface interactions.
subject: physics

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: Although both statements are true, the self-adjusting nature of static friction doesn't explain why its coefficient is higher than kinetic friction's coefficient.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming any two correct statements must have a causal relationship

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is a correct factual statement: static friction does self-adjust between zero and μₛN.
The Assertion is correct: μₖ < μₛ is a well-established experimental fact for all known surface pairs.

question_text:
Match the following types of friction (Column I) with their characteristics (Column II):

Column I:
A. Static friction
B. Kinetic friction
C. Rolling friction
D. Limiting friction

Column II:
1. Smallest among all types of contact friction
2. Has a constant value independent of applied force once motion begins
3. Maximum value of static friction at the point of impending motion
4. Self-adjusting force that opposes tendency of relative motion
explanation: A-4: Static friction is a self-adjusting force that matches the applied force up to its maximum value. B-2: Kinetic friction has a constant value (μₖN) once the body starts moving, independent of the applied force. C-1: Rolling friction is the smallest type of contact friction, which is why wheels reduce the effort needed for transport. D-3: Limiting friction is the maximum value of static friction, occurring at the point when the body is about to move.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: This matches Static friction (A) with '3' (maximum value at impending motion), but that describes limiting friction specifically, not static friction in general.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing static friction with its maximum value (limiting friction)

elimination_hints.hint: This matches Kinetic friction (B) with '1' (smallest friction), but rolling friction is the smallest, not kinetic friction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking kinetic friction is smaller than rolling friction

elimination_hints.hint: This incorrectly assigns self-adjusting behavior to kinetic friction and constant value to static friction, which is the opposite of reality.

question_text:
Match the following scenarios (Column I) with the direction of friction (Column II):

Column I:
A. A block sliding down a rough incline
B. A block being pushed up a rough incline
C. A car accelerating on a rough road
D. A car braking on a rough road

Column II:
1. Friction acts up the incline
2. Friction acts down the incline
3. Friction acts forward (direction of motion)
4. Friction acts backward (opposing motion)
explanation: A-1: When sliding down, friction opposes downward motion and acts up the incline. B-2: When being pushed up, friction opposes upward motion and acts down the incline (note: this is incorrect on second thought — friction always opposes relative motion, so if block moves up, friction acts down). Actually B-1 as well? No: for B, the block moves up, so friction acts down the incline = 2. But wait, we need unique matches. A-1: correct. B-2: Friction opposes upward motion by acting down the incline. C-3: The driving wheels push backward on the road; friction pushes the car forward. D-4: When braking, wheels slow down while the car moves forward, so friction at the braking wheels acts backward.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: This swaps the car scenarios: an accelerating car needs forward friction (3) to move, not backward (4). Friction acts forward when the car is driven.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking friction always opposes the car's motion even when accelerating

elimination_hints.hint: A block sliding down has friction acting up the incline (1), not down (2). Friction opposes the direction of sliding.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing friction direction on incline

elimination_hints.hint: The matching A-1 and C-4 conflict: for block sliding down, friction is up the incline (correct), but for an accelerating car, friction is forward (3), not backward (4).

question_text: The force of friction between two surfaces is independent of the apparent area of contact between them.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. According to the laws of friction (Amontons' laws), the frictional force is proportional to the normal force and is independent of the apparent area of contact. This may seem counterintuitive, but the actual microscopic contact area depends on the normal force, not the apparent area.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: The statement is a well-established law of friction. While intuitively one might think more area means more friction, the actual contact area at the microscopic level depends on normal force, not macroscopic area.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking friction depends on apparent contact area

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable.
This option is not applicable.

question_text: Rolling friction is greater than sliding friction for the same pair of surfaces and normal force.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. Rolling friction is much smaller than sliding (kinetic) friction. This is the fundamental reason why wheels were invented — rolling an object requires much less force than sliding it. The order is: rolling friction < kinetic friction < static friction (limiting).
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: Rolling friction is the least among all types of friction. This is why wheel-based transport is far more efficient than dragging.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking rolling friction is larger than sliding friction

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable.
This option is not applicable.

question_text: On an inclined plane, the angle at which a body just begins to slide is called the angle of _____. At this angle, the tangent of the angle equals the coefficient of _____ friction.
explanation: The angle of repose is the angle of inclination at which a body just begins to slide down. At this angle, the gravitational component along the plane equals the maximum static friction: mg sin θ = μₛ mg cos θ, giving tan θ = μₛ. Hence the angle of repose equals the angle of friction, and its tangent equals the coefficient of static friction.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: While 'angle of friction' is a related term, the specific angle of an inclined plane at which sliding begins is called the angle of repose. Also, it relates to static (not kinetic) friction.
The first blank is correct (repose), but at the angle of repose, the body is about to start sliding, so it relates to static friction, not kinetic.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing static and kinetic friction at the point of impending motion

elimination_hints.hint: The second blank (static) is correct, but the specific term for the angle of an incline at which a body starts sliding is 'angle of repose', not just 'angle of friction'.

question_text:
A delivery worker needs to push a 50 kg crate across a warehouse floor. The coefficient of static friction is 0.6 and the coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.4. The worker initially applies a gradually increasing horizontal force.

What is the minimum force needed to start the crate moving, and what force is needed to keep it moving at constant velocity? (Take g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: To start the crate moving, the applied force must overcome the maximum static friction: Fₛ = μₛ × mg = 0.6 × 50 × 10 = 300 N. Once moving at constant velocity (zero acceleration), the applied force equals kinetic friction: Fₖ = μₖ × mg = 0.4 × 50 × 10 = 200 N. The force needed to start motion is always greater than the force to maintain constant velocity.
subject: physics

options.text: 300 N to start, 300 N to maintain
200 N to start, 200 N to maintain
300 N to start, 200 N to maintain
200 N to start, 300 N to maintain

elimination_hints.hint: 300 N is correct for starting, but once moving, kinetic friction (μₖmg = 200 N) replaces static friction. Less force is needed to maintain constant velocity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the same force is needed to start and maintain motion

elimination_hints.hint: 200 N is less than the maximum static friction (300 N), so this force would not be sufficient to start the crate moving.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using kinetic friction coefficient for starting motion

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the two forces. Static friction (to start) is always greater than kinetic friction (to maintain), not the other way around.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking more force is needed to maintain motion than to start it
